metadata.title: Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Distinct Groups of Twitter Users

abstract: Online social media movements are now common and support cultural discussions on difficult health and social topics. The #MeToo movement, focusing on the pervasiveness of sexual assault and harassment, has been one of the largest and most influential online movements. Our study examines topics of conversation on Twitter by supporters of the #MeToo movement and by Twitter users who were uninvolved in the movement to explore the extent to which tweet topics for these two groups converge over time. We identify and collect one year's worth of tweets for supporters of the #MeToo movement (N = 168 users; N = 105,538 tweets) and users not involved in the movement (N = 147 users; N = 112,301 tweets referred to as the Neutral Sample). We conduct topic frequency analysis and implement an unsupervised machine learning topic modeling algorithm, latent Dirichlet allocation, to explore topics of discussion on Twitter for these two groups of users before and after the initial #MeToo movement. Our results suggest that supporters of #MeToo discussed different topics compared to the Neutral Sample of Twitter users before #MeToo with some overlap on politics. The

sections.paper_id: Baik et al. - 2022 - Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Dist
paragraphs: Social media use has grown exponentially and is becoming the standard means of communication for large segments of the population. According to Pew Research, over 72% of Americans use at least one social media platform regularly. Beyond connecting with friends, family, and co-workers, these platforms are now increasingly where Americans turn for information (Pew Research Center, 2019). Social networking sites have also emerged as a critical venue for political debate and discussion, as well as a tool to spread awareness for social issues and to generate social activism. Increasingly recent social movements, collective efforts by groups to bring about social, political, or cultural change, have turned to online activism using social media platforms to amplify the movements' messages and to reach a boarder audience.

Modern social movements, such as #BlackLivesMatter, #MeToo, and #MAGA, have used hashtag activism on social media platforms to generate a flurry of activity which in turn garners traditional media attention and brings about more awareness to the movements. While hashtag activism occurs within the Internet, these activities are often extensions of existing or brewing movements. For example, the conversations and organizing activities around BlackLivesMatter were first coined around the acquittal of George Zimmerman in 2013-a year before the hashtag went viral in the events related to the fatal shooting of Michael Brown and ensuing riots in Ferguson, Mo. The media attention on the trending hashtag in 2014 is what made #BlackLivesMatter a household name. The traditional media attention allowed more supporters to find each other online and further amplified the message of #BlackLivesMatter.

According to a recent study, 67% of U.S. adults believe that social media sites are at least somewhat important for creating sustained movements for social change (Anderson et al., 2018). Nevertheless, the question remains whether online activists, their discussions, and their communities change the overall discourse on topics related to the movement or solely help to connect like-minded individuals to share and support each other. Although both of these outcomes are important, the role of social media in propelling longterm change relies on bringing on new supporters of the movement and bridging networks. Moreover, experts believe that most social media content is produced by a minority of users, and only 14% of Americans have ever taken part in any online social movement (Huges & Wojcik, 2019). Thus, activism in social media, like all activism, may not translate to cultural change without bringing along large swaths of the population. These facts beget the question of whether online movements reach broad audiences and what we can learn about the movement's progress from posts of those connected to the movement versus those who are not.

The present study focuses on the #MeToo movement and aims to explore the extent to which the #MeToo movement's message spread to those outside the initial movement. As one of the most significant social movements to date, #MeToo focused on bringing more awareness to the pervasiveness of sexual assault and harassment. We conduct an exploratory study of Twitter discussion topic around sexual assault and harassment. We identify two groups of Twitter users: one that is connected to the #MeToo movement (publicly shared incidents of sexual assault or harassment on Twitter during the peak of the #Metoo movement), and the other is not, to explore the extent to which their tweet topics converge over time regarding sexual assault and harassment. We are interested in empirically assessing whether and the extent to which those who were not previously discussing issues of sexual assault and harassment begin to take up these topics and actively discuss them after #Metoo. The #MeToo movement's rapid, distinct, and spontaneous timing on social media allows us to clearly demark tweets before and after the start of the online movement. We examine the conversations and the users' interests in different topics before and after the #MeToo online movement to detect changes in the conversation topics. The aim is to assess shifts in the conversation topics #MeToo empirically especially around sexual assault and harassment, across the two groups.

paragraphs: The public discourse on sexual assault and harassment considerably changed on October 15, 2017, when actress Alyssa Milano called upon her followers to post "MeToo" if they had ever experienced unwanted or inappropriate sexual contact (Sini, 2017). Though the idea behind #MeToo was originated by Tarana Burke many years earlier, Ms. Milano's tweet immediately went viral because of her extensive network of followers. It garnered over 1.5 million tweets posted in the first week (Modrek & Chakalov, 2019). The response exposed the rampant and often decades-suppressed cases of sexual assault and harassment. Many of the events revealed on Twitter describe trauma and the enduring consequences that followed (Bogen et al.,20 1). Many people that had never experienced sexual assault or harassment were confronted with the unsettling knowledge of knowing someone in their network who had.

Traditional media outlets, such as Time Magazine, celebrated the outspokenness with predictions of substantial cultural and policy shifts to come (Edwards, 2017). However, almost immediately, there was evidence of a backlash brewing. There were quiet complaints that #MeToo was used to silence normal debate on a charged topic. Surveys of men revealed that they feared accusations of sexual assault and harassment and felt that the penalties, often severe career damage, were out of proportion for the acts (GQ Editors, 2018). 1 Within a year, a large proportion (40%) of the American public thought that the #MeToo movement went too far (Tolentino, 2018).

The attitudes about #MeToo were particularly troubling within workplaces. For example, studies of hiring practices found that the key takeaway message of #MeToo was to avoid hiring, mentoring, or working closely with women (Bower, 2019). A study of women comparing their workplace experiences in 2018 to 2016 found some evidence of reduced sexual harassment but more gender harassment (Keplinger et al., 2019).

#MeToo clearly provided a novel outlet to express long-held frustrations and went on to spark an international outcry and movement. If based solely on the number of social media posts it would seem that the outpour alone would lead to widespread changes in attitudes about sexual assault and harassment. However, it is important to look beyond the number of posts to understand the reach of movement or lack thereof.

sections.paper_id: Baik et al. - 2022 - Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Dist
paragraphs: Exploring whether there were sustained conversations around sexual assault and harassment on social media in the aftermath of #MeToo for supporters and those not involved in the initial conversations is the overarching aim of this study. We aim to examine the "spillover" of the #MeToo movement or changes in social media posts about sexual abuse and harassment beyond that of the initial movement supporters. We consider a spillover event when those who were not previously discussing issues of sexual abuse and assault take up these topics and actively join the online conversation about this topic. We examine topics of conversation on Twitter by supporters of the movement and by Twitter users who were uninvolved in the movement to see if any patterns of topics discussed converge, especially topics related to the #MeToo movement.

In particular, we focus on tweet topics of two consistent groups of users that either participated in the #MeToo movement or another group of Twitter users that was not involved in the movement. We examine tweet topics both before and after the #MeToo movement. We aim to explore two related questions: (a) Did the topics of discussion on Twitter differ before the #MeToo movement for the supporters and those not connected to the #MeToo movement? and (b) After the initial outpouring of the #Metoo movement, was there an increase in the discussion of topics related to sexual assault and harassment from either the supporters or those uninvolved in #MeToo?

paragraphs: The data for this project are tweets or short messages sent from a Twitter handle to its network of followers. A Twitter handle is a user's chosen Twitter username for a specific account and are unique. While it is possible for a Twitter user to have multiple Twitter handles, we will use the term Twitter user as synonymous for handle for the data in this project. We use Twitter because the #MeToo movement began on that website. Around 22% of U.S. adults use Twitter, and the site is used more for sharing news than other social media sites. While Twitter users tend to be younger, more educated, more democratic, and wealthier than U.S. adults overall, 42% of Twitter users say that they use the site to discuss politics at least some of the time (Huges & Wojcik, 2019). Importantly, Twitter data are considered existing data in the public domain and, therefore, they are exempt from human subject review.

sections.paper_id: Baik et al. - 2022 - Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Dist
paragraphs: The data collection aimed to capture data to compare the tweet topics posted from two distinct groups of Twitter users: one where sexual assault and harassment issues were salient, and a second selected to be a neutral comparison. The first group included 200 randomly sampled Twitter users from a list of 1,168 Twitter users who posted a novel (user-generated), English language, geotagged tweet in the United States that included details of sexual assault or harassment between October 15 and 21, 2017 (see Modrek & Chakalov, 2019 for detail on this sample). This is the group that we consider to be supporters of the initial #MeToo movement because they revealed an incident of sexual assault or harassment during the first week of the movement as was called on by the initial tweet from Alyssa Milano. The second group would ideally be a random sample of Twitter users posting on in the same time period as the first. However, it is impossible to recover a random sample of Twitter users as there is no roster. As an alternative, we sampled Twitter users who were active in the week before #MeToo. We chose the week before to ensure that we were not capturing users who were drawn to Twitter because of the media attention on #MeToo. We identified 1,000 Twitter users who posted a novel (user-generated), English language, U.S. geotagged tweet between October 8 and 14, 2017, the week before the start of the #MeToo movement. From this list of users, we sampled 200 Twitter users. This initial sampling for both groups was done using a Twitter developed python package (Search Tweets) and the Twitter Premium API service which is paid service that provides query-based access to Twitter's tweet archive.

For these 400 Twitter users, we collected their full timeline of tweets between April 1, 2017, and March 31, 2018, using the R package rtweet (Kearney, 2019). We extracted all tweets six months before and after #MeToo to capture discussion topics for the one-year period around #MeToo. All tweets posted between October 8 and 21, 2017, were deleted so as not to influence the topics of discussion because the events and related tweets in that period were the basis of how the Twitter users were sampled. 2 After removing tweets from those dates, our datasets include 105,538 tweets for the 168 Twitter users in the group that posted during the initial #MeToo movement, referred to as the "support sample" (SS). Our dataset also includes 112,301 tweets for the 147 Twitter users who were active on Twitter the week before #MeToo, referred to as the "Neutral Sample" (NS). Note that we reduce the number of unique Twitter users from 200 in each group to 168 and 147. This reduction is because some Twitter users only tweeted in the period 8-21 October from which the Twitter users were sampled. This does lead to some systematic bias since we are capturing the tweets and discussion topics of people from both groups who are relatively active on Twitter.

Given the sampling and the #MeToo movement's timing, we created two datasets denoting the period before and after the #MeToo movement for each group of users. The before and after SS datasets consisted of novel tweets from April 1 to October 8, 2017, and from October 21, 2017 to March 31, 2018. Novel tweets are user-generated tweets that exclude replies to other's tweets, retweets without any comment, and links to other sites or images. This was chosen to capture posts that were tied to the specific user and that would be available to all of one's followers. The six-month time period before was to ensure that we obtained sufficient information about the Twitter users' topics of discussion even before #MeToo.

The data collection aimed to capture data to compare the tweet topics posted from two distinct groups of Twitter users: one where sexual assault and harassment issues were salient, and a second selected to be a neutral comparison. The first group included 200 randomly sampled Twitter users from a list of 1,168 Twitter users who posted a novel (user-generated), English language, geotagged tweet in the United States that included details of sexual assault or harassment between October 15 and 21, 2017 (see Modrek & Chakalov, 2019 for detail on this sample). This is the group that we consider to be supporters of the initial #MeToo movement because they revealed an incident of sexual assault or harassment during the first week of the movement as was called on by the initial tweet from Alyssa Milano. The second group would ideally be a random sample of Twitter users posting on in the same time period as the first. However, it is impossible to recover a random sample of Twitter users as there is no roster. As an alternative, we sampled Twitter users who were active in the week before #MeToo. We chose the week before to ensure that we were not capturing users who were drawn to Twitter because of the media attention on #MeToo. We identified 1,000 Twitter users who posted a novel (user-generated), English language, U.S. geotagged tweet between October 8 and 14, 2017, the week before the start of the #MeToo movement. From this list of users, we sampled 200 Twitter users. This initial sampling for both groups was done using a Twitter developed python package (Search Tweets) and the Twitter Premium API service which is paid service that provides query-based access to Twitter's tweet archive.

For these 400 Twitter users, we collected their full timeline of tweets between April 1, 2017, and March 31, 2018, using the R package rtweet (Kearney, 2019). We extracted all tweets six months before and after #MeToo to capture discussion topics for the one-year period around #MeToo. All tweets posted between October 8 and 21, 2017, were deleted so as not to influence the topics of discussion because the events and related tweets in that period were the basis of how the Twitter users were sampled. 2 After removing tweets from those dates, our datasets include 105,538 tweets for the 168 Twitter users in the group that posted during the initial #MeToo movement, referred to as the "support sample" (SS). Our dataset also includes 112,301 tweets for the 147 Twitter users who were active on Twitter the week before #MeToo, referred to as the "Neutral Sample" (NS). Note that we reduce the number of unique Twitter users from 200 in each group to 168 and 147. This reduction is because some Twitter users only tweeted in the period 8-21 October from which the Twitter users were sampled. This does lead to some systematic bias since we are capturing the tweets and discussion topics of people from both groups who are relatively active on Twitter.

Given the sampling and the #MeToo movement's timing, we created two datasets denoting the period before and after the #MeToo movement for each group of users. The before and after SS datasets consisted of novel tweets from April 1 to October 8, 2017, and from October 21, 2017 to March 31, 2018. Novel tweets are user-generated tweets that exclude replies to other's tweets, retweets without any comment, and links to other sites or images. This was chosen to capture posts that were tied to the specific user and that would be available to all of one's followers. The six-month time period before was to ensure that we obtained sufficient information about the Twitter users' topics of discussion even before #MeToo.

sections.paper_id: Baik et al. - 2022 - Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Dist
paragraphs: We first preprocessed the content of the tweets and used regular expressions (patterns that describe certain sets of text) to filter out retweets, at signs, URLs, times, dates, hashtags, and characters that were not natural language texts. We then filtered out stop words, which are words that provide no information toward the analysis, for instance, "the," "to," and "a." All remaining typos, misspellings, and slang remained intact. We then tokenized or separated the text into individual words (unigrams) and two consecutive words (bigrams). Bigrams are often used in text mining to uncover a topic or theme (Silge & Robinson, 2017).

We used the tweets in two distinct ways. First, we examined common bigrams to explore the tweet topics in the data. We use bigrams, instead of higher order N-grams (i.e., trigrams), because tweets are relatively short texts. Moreover, unigrams or single words provide too little information to determine a topic. For example, the word "gun" is in both "gun control" and "gun rights." We need to examine the entire bigram, not the single word "gun," to interpret the true meaning of the topic. Second, we employed the latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic modeling, an unsupervised machine learning technique that is used to investigate the "hidden" thematic structure of a given collection of texts, on unigrams (Maier et al., 2018;Silge & Robinson, 2017). For topic modeling, we reorganized the data and combined all tweets by each Twitter user. Each composite of tweets per user was considered a document, which represented a mixture of latent topics or themes discussed by an individual Twitter user. We examine the list of topics before and after #MeToo for the two groups of Twitter users.

sections.paper_id: Baik et al. - 2022 - Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Dist
Baik et al. - 2022 - Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Dist
paragraphs: To descriptively explore the topics of discussion by the two user groups, we first conducted bigram analysis using the R programming language (package tidytext; Silge and Robinson, 2016). We compared the most common bigrams, or ones with the largest number of occurrences, before the #MeToo movement across the two groups of Twitter users to explore our first question. To create a more succinct list, we examined all the bigrams to create topic grouping that closely resembled each other. For example, "donald trump," "Xtrump," "Xtherealdonaldtrump," and "Xpresident" were combined into one topic "Donald Trump."

Our heuristic method required that we individually review each bigram. First, we totaled the frequency or occurrence of each bigram and then ranked them in descending order. Once the bigrams were ordered, we reviewed them and determined whether particular bigrams could be groups together as a topic (Appendix A has a full list of the terms and their groups, available online). If the bigram appeared at the top of the list, or had a large frequency, we determined that the topic was tweeted about frequently; specifically, we believed that these bigrams might require a closer inspection. Therefore, we first focused on comparing what the top 10 topics with the highest frequency of occurrence was across the two groups before #Metoo.

Next, we examined changes in topics of discussion. We first pulled out the top 100 bigrams topics with the highest total frequency of occurrences from SS and chose 9 bigram topics that were included both in the before and after SS datasets. These nine topics were chosen because bigrams with the highest frequency indicate their salience or importance to SS throughout the entire study period. We added a 10th topic, which was sexual assault and harassment-related tweets, because this is the focus of our study. For these 10 selected bigram topics, we compared the relative frequency of these topics before and after #MeToo for both users. We graphed the proportion of these 10 selected topics as a proportion of the total number of bigram occurrences in the top 100 bigram topics. Note that the topics were chosen to reflect the topics of salience to the SS group. There were several topics that were salient to SS and that were not in the top 100 topics of the NS.

These simple bigram analyses did not take into account the frequency of tweets by a single user. To better understand the number of users who tweeted about sexual assault topics before and after #MeToo, we assessed the proportion of users in each group that tweeted about sexual assault topics by month. We excluded the two weeks used in sampling the data in October because 100% of the SS tweet about the topic in those weeks.

sections.paper_id: Baik et al. - 2022 - Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Dist
paragraphs: Our goal with topic modeling was to examine the topics discussed by the SS and compare the topics to those discussed by the NS, before and after the #MeToo movement, using an automated method. We used the LDA algorithm, a text data mining and unsupervised statistical machine learning algorithm to build the topic model (Blei et al., 2003). The machine learning algorithm forms topics around co-occurring words that appear together in many documents; as we previously mentioned, a document in this case, refers to a composite of tweets by each Twitter user. The cluster of co-occurring words form a "topic" or theme and documents can be characterized as a "mixture of topics." The LDA algorithm's output is a list of co-occurring words that form a latent topic. The interpretation of the latent topic, based on the words, is done by the authors.

In order to do topic modeling with the LDA method, tweets were organized into documents by Twitter users by using dplyr Package in R (Wickham et al., 2020). This distinction was important because the bigram analysis examined the frequency of topics at the tweet level and not at the user level. For example, if a small number of active Twitter users discussed sexual assault and harassment, but the discussion was limited to these users only, the bigram analysis and LDA would give differing results.

To implement the LDA algorithm, users must specify several parameters optimize the output. We experimented with two parameters to maximize interpretability. First, we filter the content of tweets by excluding the terms that were either very rare or very common in the tweets using different pairs of minimum and maximum occurrences in the documents. After experimenting with several cut-offs, we used the minimum and maximum document frequencies of 0.25 and 0.75, respectively. In our case, these cut-offs mean that if a term was used by more than 75% of Twitter users or in less than 25% of Twitter users, then that term was not included in the LDA algorithm. Second, we experimented with the number of latent topics, varying from 3 to 5. After examining the output, we directed the algorithm to return groups in four main topics. We implemented these parameters using the LDA algorithm in Python using scikit-learn library to find the top four clusters of topics among the filtered unigrams across the four datasets. The algorithm then returns a term-topic matrix which is a list of words clustered into topics (see Appendix C, available online). The authors then reviewed the outputted list of words for each topic and gave each topic a heuristic name.

sections.paper_id: Baik et al. - 2022 - Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Dist
Baik et al. - 2022 - Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Dist
paragraphs: To examine our first question, whether Twitter users who supported the #MeToo movement (SS) were similar to a neutral sample of Twitter users (NS), we examined the topics of the Twitter post before the #MeToo movement. The top 10 highest frequency bigram topics, in both the before NS and SS, are shown as a percentage of the total frequency of the top 10 highest frequency bigram topics in Figure 2. Both groups of Twitter users were posting in reaction to current events and sending greetings to their followers (i.e., Happy Birthday, Merry Christmas). However, even though both groups experienced the same external events (i.e., Hurricane Maria in Puerto Rico in September 2017; Las Vegas Shooting on October 1, 2017), the topics and the relative frequencies of their tweets were systematically different. Of the top 10 bigrams "topics," there were four topics in common, "healthcare," "happy birthday," "donald trump," and "puerto rico." Notably, the proportions of even these common topics were different. For the four overlapping topics the SS users discussed "healthcare" (19% vs 6%; Fisher's exact P-value < .001) and "puerto rico" (11% vs 8%; Fisher's exact P-value < .001) more often, "happy birthday" less often (6% vs 28%; Fisher's exact P-value < .001), and "donald trump" about the same (15% vs 14% Fisher's exact P-value < .36) as the NS.

To examine our second question, we examined changes in topics comparing both groups before and after #MeToo. We chose the 10 bigram topics that SS discussed both before and after #MeToo. We used these topics for both the SS and the NS. These topics had lower bigram frequencies for the NS overall. We compared proportions of the bigrams of these 10 topics out of the top 100 bigrams before and after #MeToo in Figure 3.

Before #MeToo, SS did discuss sexual assault and harassment and this topic was in the 100 topics ranking and ranked as the 76th most common bigram. For the SS Twitter users, bigram topics encompassing sexual assault and harassment increased 13.7-fold as a proportion of topics in the top 100 in the six months after the initial #MeToo movement (from 0.04% to 5.5%, Fisher's exact P-value < .001). For the NS Twitter users, sexual assault and harassment were not in the top 100 bigram topics before #MeToo. This bigram topic did increase in the post period, reaching 1.5% of the topics in the top 100 bigram topics in the six months after the initial #MeToo movement. We could not compare the pre versus post period for this group of Twitter Note. Novel tweets are user-generated tweets that exclude replies to other's tweets, retweets without comment, and links to other sites or images. This was chosen to capture posts that were tied to the specific user and that would be available to all of one's followers. A timeline is a list of tweets posted by each user. users (Appendix B shows the 25 topics for both groups before and after #MeToo, available online).

As a comparison, we also looked at the "gun control" topic and examined changes in the frequency of discussion on this topic. The Las Vegas mass shootings that happened on October 1, 2017 is an example of an event that occurred around the time of the pre and post periods of #MeToo. sections.paper_id: Baik et al. - 2022 - Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Dist
paragraphs: Table 1 presents the heuristic titles for the four-topic LDA model. The detailed topic-term matrix is presented in Appendix C (available online). Based on the word groupings from the model, the NS discussion topic included banter, some posts with negative racial and gendered comments, and some politics and sports both before and after the #MeToo movement. Both before and after #MeToo, the topics of discussion for the NS included a negative gendered term (b***h). In contrast, the SS topics encompassed presidential politics, banter, current events and Puerto Rico before the #MeToo movement. Six months after they had many of the same topics, but the topic on presidential politics was much more gendered and racialized. The words "women" and "black" were now in this grouping. Overall in all the word groupings, three of the four topics included the term "women" in the list of most salient terms for the topics for SS in the six months after #MeToo.

sections.paper_id: Baik et al. - 2022 - Social Media Activism and Convergence in Tweet Topics After the Initial #MeToo Movement for Two Dist
paragraphs: We explore tweet topics six months before and after the #MeToo movement to understand the relative convergence on sexual assault and harassment topics by two distinct groups of Twitter users. Given the data before the #MeToo movement, we observe both similarities and differences in the topics that were salient to the two groups prior to the movement. Importantly, we observe changes in the prominence of topics related to sexual assault and compare with other changes to understand whether social media movements reach audiences with relatively little to no participation in the movement. It is crucial to understand the extent to which social media movements create support networks or whether they connect different groups of users by bridging networks and shifting the general perceptions and conversations on a topic.

In both the bigram and LDA analysis, we initially observed that the SS and NS discussed different topics before #MeToo, suggesting that the SS and NS consisted of users with distinct interests. Nonetheless, we observed that both groups engaged in political discussions and current events topics to some extent, such as Donald Trump. Further, we found that even before the #MeToo movement, the SS was discussing issues around sexual assault to some degree. Given this information, we compared differences in topics after the movement.

Interestingly, both groups increased their discussions of sexual assault and harassment after #MeToo based on the bigram topics, though at different rates. SS was already discussing sexual assault and harassment issues, and this discussion increased substantially afterward. This increase in discussions suggests that the members of SS were already invested in this topic and the #MeToo movement provided a platform to further engage in the topic. However, for the NS, sexual assault and harassment were not a key topic before #MeToo, but there appeared to be slight increases in the discussions afterward. However, the results from the LDA analysis did not support a convergence of topics. Since the analysis did not support the convergence, we believe the Neutral Sample's increase in mentions of sexual assault suggests that the #MeToo movement did increase awareness and participation among those already sympathetic to the movement but might not have enlisted a new set of supporters. Supporters of #MeToo increased their discussion of topics relevant to women significantly more in the six months after #MeToo, while the NS did not change discussion topics. The #MeToo movement did not significantly shift discussions about the movement in the NS. For this specific case, the data show that social media movements may create support networks for specific groups of supporters but may not bridge networks. These mixed results for the NS suggest that the analysis of the word count and number of tweets provide only a partial picture of the interests and engagements of the NS in the #MeToo movement. While there is some evidence of spillovers at the tweet level, there is limited evidence of convergence at the user level. A contribution of this work is to highlight the diversity of experiences that online movements, like #MeToo, engender. A key difference in social media versus traditional media is that the information presented is highly individualized. Algorithms favor showing users posts that are from those they follow or those with whom they have previously shared similar opinions. Once a user engages in a social media conversation and/or uses hashtag, such as members our SS, the algorithm learns their interests and gives the user even more similar posts to view. Therefore, there is the possibility, especially in charged discourse about sexual misconduct, of intensification of feelings, support, and beliefs about a movement amongst supporters that propels the conversation to continue. On the other side, there are users for whom a particular movement is presented but not emphasized because they do not engage with the post/topic/information. The underlying algorithms learn that this is not a topic of interest. Therefore, neither sets of social media users see the counterperspective on how engaged and unengaged users experience the online movement. That is why analyses, like ours, that attempt to compare discussion convergence on movement topics by distinct groups are critical for understanding the longer term changes that the online movement may inspire offline.

A key strength of our study is the size of our data and the innovation of analysis methods. First, our analysis included over 200,000 tweets from approximately 300 users that are classified into two consistent groups of users over two consistent time periods. Therefore, we know that these two users are experiencing the same external events and can put their tweets into a broader context. We know of no other study that uses a consistent comparison group of Twitter users before and after a social media event. Second, our method uses both human annotators and machine learning algorithms that examine underlying topics (LDA topic models). We believe that both are needed to understand social media data. However, given the rise of prominence in social media data, the methods used here will be increasingly useful in understanding how social movements spread and sustain themselves after the initial events on social media platforms.

Despite these strengths, there are critical limitations to this study. First, without a full roster of all Twitter users or tweets within a certain period, it is impossible to sample a random sample of Twitter users, which would have been the gold standard comparison group relative to SS. Instead, we argue that the NS consists of Twitter users that were active in approximately the same time period and therefore serve as an appropriate comparison group to SS.

Since the NS was not selected based on any term or keyword, but rather when the users posted the tweets on Twitter, one clear bias in using this method is that any user that tweets more frequently will be more likely to be selected. This nonrandom selection is evident when we compare the frequency of tweets between the two groups. The NS has a higher number of mean and median tweets than SS. Moreover, any event that selects for a particular interest that was more salient to a particular population in the week before #MeToo would be oversampled (i.e., 2017 Major League Baseball playoffs would lead to more Twitter users interested in Baseball).

An alternative selection procedure for creating a comparison group would have been to select a demographically matched group who did not participate in the #MeToo movement. However, it is not possible to sample Twitter users based on gender, age, and/or race using the Twitter API. We know from previous research that SS consisted of predominantly white women (Modrek & Chakalov, 2019). However, we do not have the demographic profile of the NS. Moreover, online movements, such as #Metoo, may not reflect the demographic diversity of the offline movement. The first week of the online #MeToo was initiated on Twitter by a white actress, and SS in this study overrepresents white women who were revealing past experiences with sexual assault. Although these women identified themselves as victims of sexual assault or harassment at some point in their lives, they may not represent all those who are victims or supporters of #MeToo. For instance, the initial "MeToo" movement was conceived of by a black woman, Tarana Burke, on MySpace as a way to empower young women of color to voice their experience with sexual violence (Edwards, 2017). Given that national statistics suggest that young women, and women of color, experience sexual assault at higher rates than other groups, our study's SS does not generalize to all victims or supporters of the message of #MeToo.

Finally, our LDA model is exploratory, and we only examine models with a limited number of topics. The LDA model is sensitive to the model's tuning parameters, which leads to a considerable difference in the results. These parameters include the number of topics based on word frequency cut-off for common and rare words. We chose model parameters to include 3-5 topics to make the outputs more interpretable. We experimented with the number of topics and found that a higher number of topics led to related topics being assigned to different groups. Moreover, the higher number of topics, the more groups of terms that were difficult to interpret. We chose to capture fewer and broader topics, rather than many subtopics. These data could be reanalyzed with different tuning parameters to answer question about particular or narrower topics.

The methods presented here could be used to study a wide array of social media movements, especially when distinct supporters and timing can be identified and used to create a before and after tweets for different groups. In the context of #MeToo, this study complements existing analyses of the aftermath of #MeToo where both positive and negative outcomes and attitudes have been found (Bogen et al., 20 1;Freelon et al., 2019;Kaufman et al. 2019;Levy & Mattsson, 2019). Within Twitter, we document more discussion of gender topics amongst supporters of #MeToo (perhaps even evidence of echo chambers on Twitter (Garimella et al., 2018) but limited discussion of sexual assault and harassment by those not engaged in the initial conversation. The lack of convergence highlights that although the tweet count suggests a watershed moment and a cultural change, the lack of change in tweet topics for those not involved may have foreshadowed the backlash that ensued.

It is crucial to understand the extent to which social media movements create support networks or whether they connect different groups of users by bridging networks and shifting the general perceptions and conversations on a topic.

In both the bigram and LDA analysis, we initially observed that the SS and NS discussed different topics before #MeToo, suggesting that the SS and NS consisted of users with distinct interests. Nonetheless, we observed that both groups engaged in political discussions and current events topics to some extent, such as Donald Trump. Further, we found that even before the #MeToo movement, the SS was discussing issues around sexual assault to some degree. Given this information, we compared differences in topics after the movement.

Interestingly, both groups increased their discussions of sexual assault and harassment after #MeToo based on the bigram topics, though at different rates. SS was already discussing sexual assault and harassment issues, and this discussion increased substantially afterward. This increase in discussions suggests that the members of SS were already invested in this topic and the #MeToo movement provided a platform to further engage in the topic. However, for the NS, sexual assault and harassment were not a key topic before #MeToo, but there appeared to be slight increases in the discussions afterward. However, the results from the LDA analysis did not support a convergence of topics. Since the analysis did not support the convergence, we believe the Neutral Sample's increase in mentions of sexual assault suggests that the #MeToo movement did increase awareness and participation among those already sympathetic to the movement but might not have enlisted a new set of supporters. Supporters of #MeToo increased their discussion of topics relevant to women significantly more in the six months after #MeToo, while the NS did not change discussion topics. The #MeToo movement did not significantly shift discussions about the movement in the NS. For this specific case, the data show that social media movements may create support networks for specific groups of supporters but may not bridge networks. These mixed results for the NS suggest that the analysis of the word count and number of tweets provide only a partial picture of the interests and engagements of the NS in the #MeToo movement. While there is some evidence of spillovers at the tweet level, there is limited evidence of convergence at the user level. A contribution of this work is to highlight the diversity of experiences that online movements, like #MeToo, engender. A key difference in social media versus traditional media is that the information presented is highly individualized. Algorithms favor showing users posts that are from those they follow or those with whom they have previously shared similar opinions. Once a user engages in a social media conversation and/or uses hashtag, such as members our SS, the algorithm learns their interests and gives the user even more similar posts to view. Therefore, there is the possibility, especially in charged discourse about sexual misconduct, of intensification of feelings, support, and beliefs about a movement amongst supporters that propels the conversation to continue. On the other side, there are users for whom a particular movement is presented but not emphasized because they do not engage with the post/topic/information. The underlying algorithms learn that this is not a topic of interest. Therefore, neither sets of social media users see the counterperspective on how engaged and unengaged users experience the online movement. That is why analyses, like ours, that attempt to compare discussion convergence on movement topics by distinct groups are critical for understanding the longer term changes that the online movement may inspire offline.

A key strength of our study is the size of our data and the innovation of analysis methods. First, our analysis included over 200,000 tweets from approximately 300 users that are classified into two consistent groups of users over two consistent time periods. Therefore, we know that these two users are experiencing the same external events and can put their tweets into a broader context. We know of no other study that uses a consistent comparison group of Twitter users before and after a social media event. Second, our method uses both human annotators and machine learning algorithms that examine underlying topics (LDA topic models). We believe that both are needed to understand social media data. However, given the rise of prominence in social media data, the methods used here will be increasingly useful in understanding how social movements spread and sustain themselves after the initial events on social media platforms.

Despite these strengths, there are critical limitations to this study. First, without a full roster of all Twitter users or tweets within a certain period, it is impossible to sample a random sample of Twitter users, which would have been the gold standard comparison group relative to SS. Instead, we argue that the NS consists of Twitter users that were active in approximately the same time period and therefore serve as an appropriate comparison group to SS.

Since the NS was not selected based on any term or keyword, but rather when the users posted the tweets on Twitter, one clear bias in using this method is that any user that tweets more frequently will be more likely to be selected. This nonrandom selection is evident when we compare the frequency of tweets between the two groups. The NS has a higher number of mean and median tweets than SS. Moreover, any event that selects for a particular interest that was more salient to a particular population in the week before #MeToo would be oversampled (i.e., 2017 Major League Baseball playoffs would lead to more Twitter users interested in Baseball).

An alternative selection procedure for creating a comparison group would have been to select a demographically matched group who did not participate in the #MeToo movement. However, it is not possible to sample Twitter users based on gender, age, and/or race using the Twitter API. We know from previous research that SS consisted of predominantly white women (Modrek & Chakalov, 2019). However, we do not have the demographic profile of the NS. Moreover, online movements, such as #Metoo, may not reflect the demographic diversity of the offline movement. The first week of the online #MeToo was initiated on Twitter by a white actress, and SS in this study overrepresents white women who were revealing past experiences with sexual assault. Although these women identified themselves as victims of sexual assault or harassment at some point in their lives, they may not represent all those who are victims or supporters of #MeToo. For instance, the initial "MeToo" movement was conceived of by a black woman, Tarana Burke, on MySpace as a way to empower young women of color to voice their experience with sexual violence (Edwards, 2017). Given that national statistics suggest that young women, and women of color, experience sexual assault at higher rates than other groups, our study's SS does not generalize to all victims or supporters of the message of #MeToo.

Finally, our LDA model is exploratory, and we only examine models with a limited number of topics. The LDA model is sensitive to the model's tuning parameters, which leads to a considerable difference in the results. These parameters include the number of topics based on word frequency cut-off for common and rare words. We chose model parameters to include 3-5 topics to make the outputs more interpretable. We experimented with the number of topics and found that a higher number of topics led to related topics being assigned to different groups. Moreover, the higher number of topics, the more groups of terms that were difficult to interpret. We chose to capture fewer and broader topics, rather than many subtopics. These data could be reanalyzed with different tuning parameters to answer question about particular or narrower topics.

The methods presented here could be used to study a wide array of social media movements, especially when distinct supporters and timing can be identified and used to create a before and after tweets for different groups. In the context of #MeToo, this study complements existing analyses of the aftermath of #MeToo where both positive and negative outcomes and attitudes have been found (Bogen et al., 20 1;Freelon et al., 2019;Kaufman et al. 2019;Levy & Mattsson, 2019). Within Twitter, we document more discussion of gender topics amongst supporters of #MeToo (perhaps even evidence of echo chambers on Twitter (Garimella et al., 2018) but limited discussion of sexual assault and harassment by those not engaged in the initial conversation. The lack of convergence highlights that although the tweet count suggests a watershed moment and a cultural change, the lack of change in tweet topics for those not involved may have foreshadowed the backlash that ensued.
sections.preview_head: We explore tweet topics six months before and after the #MeToo movement to understand the relative convergence on sexual assault and harassment topics by two distinct groups of Twitter users. Given the data before the #MeToo movement, we observe both similarities and differences in the topics that were salient to the two groups prior to the movement. Importantly, we observe changes in the prominence of topics related to sexual assault and compare with other changes to understand whether social media movements reach audiences with relatively little to no participation in the movement.
